metadata.title: The Spinning Compass Inside Every MRI

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Merhaba, Liam — in for Bear. What fixes that rate, and why does the z-component stay frozen? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: What fixes that rate, and why does the z-component stay frozen??
props.segment: The Spinning Compass Inside Every MRI

beats.narration_text: Every MRI scanner works by listening. Millions of proton spins inside you are circling at the same frequency — and the scanner picks up that exact rotation.

card.copy: The Spinning Compass Inside Every MRI

beats.narration_text: Drop a proton into a magnetic field pointing up. The proton's spin arrow tilts at some angle — and then, instead of staying still, it starts to circle. Like a tilted top that won't fall.
The z-component stays exactly fixed the whole time. Only the horizontal part rotates. The spin is on a circle at constant latitude — precessing.
The Question: the z-component of the spin never changes. Yet the spin is clearly doing something — circling at a constant rate. What fixes that rate, and why does the z-component stay frozen?

card.copy: Lz is frozen. The azimuth rotates. What sets the rate, and why the split?

beats.narration_text: Naive picture: the field should push the spin to align — either all the way up, or all the way down. Classical magnets align. Why does the quantum spin just circle instead?

The mechanism: the field makes each spin eigenstate advance its phase at a different rate. The up-state gains phase at one speed, the down-state at another. Their relative phase is the azimuth on the Bloch sphere — and that relative phase ticks forward at their difference.

The rate of that advance is the Larmor frequency: omega equals gamma times B. Gamma is the gyromagnetic ratio — it depends only on the particle. B is the field strength. Double the field, double the frequency.

Why does Lz stay fixed? The phase advance on each eigenstate doesn't change the probability of finding the spin up or down — only the relative phase between them changes. The polar angle is unchanged. Only the azimuth moves.

In a 1.5 Tesla clinical MRI scanner, protons precess at 63.9 megahertz — 64 million circles per second. At 3 Tesla, the rate doubles to 127.7 megahertz. The scanner broadcasts a radio pulse at exactly that frequency, and the spins answer.

props.headline: In a 1.5 Tesla clinical MRI scanner, protons precess at 63.9…

beats.scene_description: Interior of a clinical MRI scanner bore, circular magnets surrounding a patient table, showing the scale and field geometry

beats.narration_text: A physicist sets up a proton with spin tilted at 60 degrees from the z-axis, inside a 1.5 Tesla field. She calculates: z-component is hbar times cosine 60, which is hbar over 2. That value never changes. Azimuth advances at 63.9 MHz. She measures: the probability of spin-up stays constant, the transverse signal oscillates at exactly 63.9 MHz.
beats.example_note: illustrative — numbers from the chapter's worked example at 1.5 T

beats.narration_text: Why does a proton spin circle in a magnetic field without ever aligning? Because the field advances the relative phase of the up and down eigenstates — moving the azimuth — while leaving the amplitudes, and therefore the z-projection, exactly unchanged.

card.copy: omega = gamma * B. The z-component is frozen. The azimuth circles.

beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. In a 1.5 Tesla clinical MRI scanner, protons precess at 63.9 megahertz — 64 million circles per second. At 3 Tesla, the rate doubles to 127.7 megahertz. The scanner broadcasts a radio pulse at exactly that frequency, and the spins answer. A physicist sets up a proton with spin tilted at 60 degrees from the z-axis, inside a 1.5 Tesla field. She calculates: z-component is hbar times cosine 60, which is hbar over 2. That value never changes. Azimuth advances at 63.9 MHz. She measures: the probability of spin-up stays constant, the transverse signal oscillates at exactly 63.9 MHz.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
